questions.question: Explain the Iterator pattern and why it is preferred over index-based iteration in Java Collections.
keyPoints: Provides uniform interface for traversing different collection types
Hides internal implementation details (array vs linked-list)
Supports fail-fast behavior for concurrent modification detection
More readable and less error-prone than manual index management
Works with collections that don't support random access (Set, Queue)

questions.question: What is ConcurrentModificationException? How does Java's fail-fast iterator detect it?
keyPoints: Thrown when collection modified during iteration
Iterator maintains modCount (modification count)
Copies modCount at iterator creation
Throws CME when modCount changes unexpectedly
Structural modifications trigger this, not element modifications

questions.question: Differentiate between Iterator, ListIterator, and Enumeration in Java.
keyPoints: Enumeration: legacy (JDK 1.0), hasMoreElements(), nextElement(), no remove()
Iterator: JDK 1.2+, hasNext(), next(), remove() - unidirectional
ListIterator: extends Iterator, bidirectional, add(), set(), hasPrevious(), previous()
ListIterator only works with List implementations

questions.question: Write Java code to remove all strings starting with 'A' from an ArrayList using Iterator.
keyPoints: Create iterator from list using list.iterator()
Use while(it.hasNext()) loop
Check condition on it.next()
Call it.remove() for matching elements
Never use list.remove() inside iterator loop

questions.question: Explain why for-each loop may not be suitable in certain scenarios where Iterator is required.
keyPoints: Cannot modify collection (add/remove) during iteration
Cannot iterate backwards
Cannot obtain index position
Cannot iterate multiple collections in parallel
Cannot use with raw types that don't implement Iterable

questions.question: Design a Java program that uses ListIterator to traverse a list both forward and backward, demonstrating add(), set(), and remove() operations.
keyPoints: Create List with elements
Obtain ListIterator from list.listIterator()
Forward traversal: hasNext(), next(), nextIndex()
Backward traversal: hasPrevious(), previous(), previousIndex()
Add element: it.add(element) inserts before cursor
Set element: it.set(element) modifies last returned element
Remove element: it.remove() removes last returned element
Show state changes after each operation

questions.question: Compare fail-fast and fail-safe iterators. Give examples of collections providing each type and explain the internal mechanisms.
keyPoints: Fail-fast: throws ConcurrentModificationException on concurrent modification
Fail-safe: operates on collection clone, no exceptions
ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet provide fail-fast iterators
CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap provide fail-safe iterators
Fail-fast uses modCount variable to track structural changes
Fail-safe creates snapshot/copy of collection at iterator creation
Trade-off: fail-fast detects problems immediately, fail-safe may miss updates
Performance: fail-safe may have higher memory overhead for cloning
